summary: Gruber critiques Twitter's experimental reply interface as an example of hiding complexity rather than encapsulating it. He argues that when users are confused by a design, the fault lies with the designers, not the users. The core distinction he draws is between sweeping complexity under a rug (hiding) versus actually resolving it (encapsulating). He references Panzarino's suggestion to simply remove @names from the character count while keeping them visible. In a footnote, he extends the principle to Apple, praising the original Mac and iOS for encapsulating complexity well, while noting that macOS's handling of file extensions is an example where Apple got it wrong.
key_points: Hiding complexity and encapsulating complexity are fundamentally different design approaches — one sweeps the mess under a rug, the other actually cleans it up
Designers who hide complexity are implicitly disdainful of their users, assuming they're too stupid to understand rather than accepting that the design itself is flawed
Twitter's new reply interface hides the mechanics of replies rather than making them genuinely understandable
Apple's original Mac succeeded not because it was for dummies but because it was the first system that wasn't confusing — smart people flocked to it
macOS's default hiding of file extensions is a case where Apple failed its own principle: if extensions are required, show them; if not, design a system that never needs them
money_quotes: The fundamental problem with most designers of complex systems intended for mass market use is that they decide to hide complexity.
They think their users are stupid, so they need to present them with a design for stupid people.
If people are confused with a design, the problem is with the design, not with the users.
It's the difference between actually cleaning up a mess versus just sweeping the mess under a rug.
A good 'simple' design will help users to understand what is actually going on, how a thing actually works.
A bad 'simple' design will leave users just as confused as ever with even less chance of figuring it out, because what they need to see to understand it is hidden.
The genius of the original Mac wasn't that it was suitable for dummies but that it was the first system that wasn't confusing.
If you're going to require file name extensions in your system, then show them.
key_insight: Good design encapsulates complexity so users genuinely understand what's happening, while bad design merely hides it, leaving users more confused with less ability to figure things out.
